meta.title: Mercedes M274 Engine — Specs, Reliability & Replacement Cost
meta.description: The M274 is a refined turbo four and Ward's 10 Best winner — but camshaft wear is a known weakness from 60k miles. See honest costs and compare specialist quotes.

@graph.name: Mercedes-Benz models fitted with the M274

mainEntity.name: How much does an M274 engine replacement cost?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned M274 typically costs £2,200–£3,800 supply only, varying by specific variant and power output. Fitting adds £800–£1,200 depending on labour rates. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your car.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes M274 a reliable engine?

acceptedAnswer.text: The M274 is rated 3.5★ out of 5 — it won Ward's 10 Best Engines in 2017 and is generally refined and efficient, but camshaft adjuster wear and timing chain tensioner issues are well-documented, especially from 60,000 miles.

mainEntity.name: What is the most common M274 failure?

acceptedAnswer.text: The most common failure is camshaft adjuster (phaser) wear, caused by a design anomaly. Symptoms include cold-start rattle, Check Engine Light, and rough running. Repair requires replacing both camshafts — a £1,000+ job at an independent specialist.

mainEntity.name: Which Mercedes models use the M274?

acceptedAnswer.text: The M274 powered a wide range of Mercedes models including the A-Class (W176), CLA-Class (C117), C-Class (W205), E-Class (W212/W213), GLA-Class (X156), GLC-Class (X253), and SLC/SLK-Class (R172), plus the Infiniti Q50 and Q60.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes M274 the same as the Infiniti version?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — Infiniti used the M274 in the Q50 and Q60 under license from Mercedes-Benz. The engine is mechanically identical, though ECU tuning differs slightly between brands. The same camshaft and timing chain issues affect both.

hero.h1: Mercedes-Benz M274 Engine — Specs, Reliability & Replacement Cost
hero.subHeadline: The Mercedes M274 is a 2.0-litre turbocharged four-cylinder — a Ward's 10 Best Engines winner, but its camshaft design is a known weakness. Backed by real UK enquiries from EnginesMarket.co.uk.

rows.metric: Confidence: 88% confidence — based on verified specialist rebuild reports and UK workshop consensus.

rows.value: Mix of cheap fixes (thermostat, sensors) and expensive ones (camshafts, timing chain)

rows.metric: Available but specialist-order only; Mercedes parts premium applies

rows.value: Recon cost 30–44% of lowest host-vehicle value

One-line verdict: The M274 is a refined, efficient turbo four when maintained — but camshaft adjuster wear and timing chain tensioner issues are well-documented from 60,000 miles — insist on full service history and listen for cold-start rattle.

rows.metric: Best for: Buyers wanting a modern, efficient Mercedes turbo petrol with strong performance.
rows.value: Avoid if: You're looking at a high-mileage example with no documented camshaft or timing chain work.
Also powers the Infiniti Q50 (2014–2022) and Q60 (2016–2022).
Labour estimate: Fitting an M274 typically takes 8–12 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer).

rows.metric: Shared-cost note: Parts cost is identical across Mercedes-Benz and Infiniti applications; labour time varies slightly by engine bay access.
